segments.text: The next two times four point three point twenty-five, monks.
This holy life is not lived to cheat after Joel people.
It is not concerned with getting gain, profit or notoriety.
It is not concerned with the flood of gossip, nor with the idea of that folks know me
as so and so.
No monks, this holy life is lived for the sake of self-restrain, for the sake of abandoning,
for the sake of detachment from the passions, for the sake of making to seize.
This is one of those who task that concern more, there are concerned with monks more.
And here the Buddha is trying to say that the main purpose of the holy life is to end
this round of rebirth and now suffering to cut off the evil.
And then once a person becomes a monk, there are many distractions if he is not careful.
Either he can become corrupt and also a Kusala and skillful actions, like not keeping
the percepts, etc.
Getting caught up with him, name and fame.
Or sometimes he might get involved with work, which is concerned with a monk.
For example, trying to propagate the Dharma, trying to teach meditation and writing books
and get caught up with funerals, chanting, etc.
But if he does too much of these things, he can become distracted from the main tasks or the
main aim of the holy life, which is to cultivate ourselves and to let go and to end
out of suffering.
So this took us more for the monks.
Now the next 2 times 4.3.28, the Buddha said, monks, these 4 are in lineage's reckoned as ancient
as of long-standing, astraditional, permeable, pure and un-undilated now as then are not confounded.
Now shall they be, are not despised by discerning recluses and brahmanas, but are the 4.
Herein monks, a monk is content with any sort of ropes and speaks in praise of such content.
For the sake of getting ropes, he resorts not to unseemly and unbecoming conduct.
Here he gets not ropes, he is not dismayed therein, and if he does get them his free from the
born of selfishness, of greed, of craving for them.
Seeing the danger therein and skilled in the escape therefrom, he makes use of them.
Here does he not exalt himself because of his content with any sort of ropes, nor does he
disparage others who are not content.
Who so, who so monks, is skilled herein, not slothful, but mindful and heedful.
This monk is one who stands firm in the primeval ancient Aryan lineage.
Then again, a monk is content with any sort of arms food and speaks in praise of such content.
For the sake of getting arms food, he resorts not to what is unseemly and unbecoming.
If he gets not arms food, he is not dismayed therein, and if he does get his free from the
born of selfishness, of greed, of craving for it.
Seeing the danger therein and skilled in the escape therefrom, he makes use of it.
Here does he not exalt himself because of his content with any sort of arms food.
Nor does he disparage others who are not content.
Who so monks is skilled herein.
This monk is one who stands firm in the primeval ancient Aryan lineage.
Then again, a monk is content with any sort of lodging and speaks in praise of such content.
For the sake of getting lodging, he resorts not, etc.
etc. If he gets not lodging, he is not dismayed therein.
Who so monks is skilled herein, stands firm in the Aryan lineage, etc.
Once more monks, a monk delights in letting go abandoning.
So delighting, he delights in developing.
Yet because of his delighting in the one abandoning and the other developing,
he exhausts not himself because of that delighting.
Nor does he disparage others who delights not therein.
Who so monks is skilled herein, not thoughtful, but mindful and heedful.
This monk is one who stands firm in the primeval ancient Aryan lineage.
These monks are the four Aryan lineage's reckon as ancient, as of longstanding,
as traditional primeval pure and unadulterated now, and stand,
which are not confounded nor shall be, are not despised by discerning
recluses and prominence. For over monks possess of these four Aryan lineage,
whether a monk dwell in the east or the west, north or south, wherever he may dwell,
he masters this content and content monks, he is a sage.
I listen to the sutra which is concerned more with monks.
I'm telling the monks not to be too concerned with ropes,
the recluses and the ropes, the food and the lodgings to be content with whatever
among its. The last one is letting go abandoning.
We shall cause among practices and stages, eventually suppose to let go of the self,
there you go. These four factors, the Buddha talks about contentment,
the contentment is one of the factors of the holy life.
There's the Buddha is supposed to be called vijacharana,
some panno replete with sarana is sometimes translated as conduct
or practice, and the other one is knowledge.
So in the practice, contentment is one of the factors,
and this contentment leads to reclessness and is one of the causes for a monk to disrobe.
The other thing that is important, the Buddha mentioned here is that if a monk is developed,
then he does not exalt himself and he does not disparage others. Even others are not up to
standard, not up to whatever standard he has in mind, he does not put down
yet a monk, I mean not on a personal level, but then the Buddha is also quite reasonable person.
For example, in the vijaya, the monk is disciplinary court, the Buddha said that if a monk does not get enough,
enough quantity of arm's food, wherever he stays, he should leave that place.
Because you can't practice the holy life and you don't get enough food.
The other thing is, in the vijaya, the Buddha also mentioned that if a monk does not
the food, he gets it's not of quality food, because if he doesn't get quality food,
then we don't have the strength to practice the holy life as well, especially if you want to stay
up at night and get up early etc. So the Buddha also said that if we don't get enough quality food,
we should leave the place also, stay there. I come to another Buddha 4.3.29.
Buddha said, monks, these four are factors of Dharma,
recognize ancient of long-standing, as traditional, primeval, pure and unadulated. Now is then
unadulated, nor shall they be unadulated by discerning, refuses and brahmanas, but
therefore non-covered thing, monks is a factor of Dharma. Non-malice, right mindfulness and right
concentration are not despised, are factors of Dharma, recklessness, ancient of long-standing,
as traditional, primeval, pure and unadulated. Now is then unadulated, nor shall they be unadulated by
discerning, refuses and brahmanas. These are the four factors of Dharma. These four things,
the first one is non-covered thing, unadulated. I mean non-greed is something that's
important for a monk. Another one is non-malice, avaya-pada. That means not to have a big temple,
not to have a lot of anger. There's another factor. This important, the third one is right mindfulness,
all right, recollection and samasakti. The fourth one is right concentration, samasamada.
You notice here as elsewhere in the sutras, that some right mindfulness and right concentration,
they are not the same thing. They are two different factors. Nowadays there's a tendency for some
people to teach that right mindfulness is enough. That right mindfulness is right concentration.
It is not. It's always two separate things in the sutras. Another sutras is four,
nine, four, point, thirty, one. monks. There are these four views possessed of which on
Davis and mankind, there are roles of four-wheeled prosperity, possessed of which both Davis and
mankind in no long time attain greatness and increase in prosperity. But are the four views?
They are dwelling in a fitting place, association with true men, perfect application of the
self and merit done in the past. These are the four views possessed of which on Davis and mankind,
there are roles of four-wheeled prosperity, possessed of which both Davis and mankind in no long time
attain greatness and increase in prosperity. This sutras which is both for monks and for lay people.
These four factors, actually they also found in the mangala sutra, the discourse on the high
blessings. The first one is in the mangala sutra is the parli, spati, rupa, de savaso, cha,
dwelling in a fitting place. What do you mean by dwelling in a fitting place?
Dwelling in a fitting place for lay people would be dwelling in a place where you can listen to the
dama. You have a chance to learn the dama from monks and a chance to serve the Sangha,
like making offerings to the Sangha, etc. For monks, it will be a place where it is conducive
for meditation, conducive for progress in the spiritual path where he can progress from
the day to day in his meditation. The second factor is in the mangala sutra is called Pandita Nanjaseva
association with truman. He actually the parli word for the truman is sapurisa,
sapurisa, truman meaning, ariasla, men who have advanced along the spiritual path.
Because if you associate with truman, then you get to listen to the true dama
and then when you get to listen to the true dama, you know how to practice
and walk the skillful path where you can minimize your suffering and
cultivate a lot of blessings that will help you to along the spiritual path and you will
help you not only this lifetime but lead you towards a good rebirth in the future life.
Third one is atasama, paneedicya, that means perfect guidance of the self or
perfect application of the self that means guiding oneself along the right path, perfect path.
And there are of course, uh, uh, and he is understanding the dama, listening to the dama.
And then the fourth one is merit done in the past, pubhiya, katapunyata.
If we have done merit in the past, then it will help us in this lifetime.
Or if we do a lot of good deeds now, it will help us in our future lifetime.
So these are the four things, if we practice them, we will get a lot of benefit this life and future
life. Dwelling in the fitting place, association with truman, perfect application of the self
and merit done in the past. The next two times four point, four point 32,
monks, there are these four bases of sympathy, but for generosity or charity, kind speech,
doing a good turn and treating all the life. These are the four, these four bases of sympathy.
If we practice them, then we can get a lot of friends. There are, there's a mentioned
some other suta where some of the Buddha's lady's idols and they had a great following
of friends. And because they practiced these four things, the first one is generosity,
nobody likes a selfish person, nobody likes to go near a selfish person. But if you are generous,
everybody will want to come around you. Second one is kind speech to be careful that we speak
kind words, but we are too hurting. Sometimes you can't help it, sometimes we have to speak
a harsh words, sometimes if you want to wake up some people, just like sometimes parents have to
spank their kids in a little while, once in a while, to get them to go along the right road.
Third one is doing a good turn, doing a good turn to others. And there's a suta elsewhere,
I think I might have gone to it. The Buddha said that it's very hard to find somebody who
do a good turn to somebody first. Normally, we only return something that's good than to us.
And it's to be the first to do a good to somebody else. It's quite rare. It's just like a
grateful person, very difficult to find in the world. And then the fourth one is treating all the
life. It won't discriminate too much, especially in terms of a race, in terms of wealth,
or etc. So these are the four basis of sympathy, generosity, kind speech, doing a good turn,
treating all the life. Now the next two times four point four point 33, the Buddha said that
months, the lion, king of beast, in the evening comes fourth from his lair, having come forth
from his lair, he stretches himself, having done so, he surveys the four quarters in all directions,
having done that, he attests, tries his lines raw, tries having uttered his lines raw,
he sells forth in search of prey. Now months whatsoever, brute creatures hear the sound of the
roaring of the lion, king of beast. For the most part, they are afraid, before to craking and
trembling, those that dwell in holes seek them, water dwellers make for the water, forest dwellers enter
the forest, birds mown into the air. Then months whatsoever, Rajas elephants, in village town,
or Rajas residents, are tethered with stout leather bonds, such birds and rain those bonds are
sunder, void their experiments and impenet run to and fro, thus potent months is the line,
king of beast, over brute creatures of such mighty power and majesty is he.
Just so months, when a tatagata arises in the world, and at a hunt, a perfectly enlightened one,
perfect in knowledge and conduct, welfareer, a world-knower, the answer past trainer of men who can
be trained, teacher of devars and mankind, a Buddha and exalted one. He teaches Dharma, such is the
being in the body, such the origin of the being in the body, such is the ending of the being in
the body, such is the practice going to end of the being in the body. Then months whatsoever,
devars there be, long lived, lovely, and become happy for a long time established in lofty palaces.
They too, on hearing the Dharma teaching of the tatagata, for the most part are afraid.
They fall to creating and trembling, saying, it seems as that we who taught ourselves
permanent are after all impermanent, that we who taught ourselves stable are after all unstable.
Not to last, it seems, are we, and lasting we taught ourselves. So it seems,
serves that we are impermanent and stable, not to last, composed with a being in the body.
Thus potent months is a tatagata over the world of devars and mankind, of such mighty power and
majesty is he. And this uta is comparing the lion and the king of the beast with the Buddha.
Among all the animals, the lion is the chief, so that even when he roars, even such a huge creature,
like Elephant will panic, avoid his experiments and burst their bones, try to run away.
So in the same way, when the Buddha teaches the Dharma, there are these devars, heavenly beings,
who have long, very long lives in a millions of years, and even though they live for millions of years,
they never each, they look like a 16 year old man or a 16 year old boy or a 16 year old girl.
And after millions of years, they still look 16 years old. So they never imagine that they're going to die.
And because the life is so blissful and happy, and they just assume that they are not going to die.
But after hearing the Dharma and after observing the Buddha, then they realize that the Buddha is
one who speaks the truth, never speaks a lie. So then they realize that they are going to die one day,
and then they get scared. Some of the sutras we've explained when Deva heavily being is about to die.
And he realizes he's about to die, he gets very worried, thoughts run very fast, very panicky,
he starts to sweat. Formerly he didn't have any body odor, he starts to have body odor.
For me, like he used to have flowers on the head, naturally the flowers were built and all that.
So when he passes away and the body just disappears, just like when he's born in the heaven,
the bodies suddenly appears. Doesn't have to go to a nine-month period like human beings in the womb.
The other thing that's mentioned here, the being in the body, Sakaya,
normally all beings, we have a view that there is a self in the body.
We attach the body, we have attachment to this body and we take it to be the self.
Then as we progress along the spiritual path and then we come to the state when you realize that the body is not the self.
But then we still cling to the mind as the self. Eventually we still have to let go of the mind.
That's who times 4.4.35. On a certain occasion the exalted one was staying near Rajagaha in the bamboo
grove at the squirrels feeding ground. On that occasion Vasakara the Brahmin, a great official of
Magda came to visit the exalted one and on coming to him greeted him continuously and after the
exchange of greetings and courtesy sat down at one side. As he sat that Vasakara the Brahmin said this to
the exalted one. Master Gautama, we Brahmins proclaim a man if he possesses four qualities as one of
great wisdom as a great man. What are the four qualities? Here in Master Gautama he is learning.
Of whatsoever he hears he understands the meaning as soon as it is uttered, saying this is the meaning
of that saying, this is the meaning of that saying. For over he has a good memory, he can remember
and recall a thing done long ago, said long ago. Again in all the business of a householder he
is killed and diligent and therein he is resourceful and capable of investigating what is proper
to be done, what should be arranged. Now Master Gautama, if a man possesses these qualities,
we proclaim him as one of great wisdom as a great man. If the worthy Gautama thinks me worthy
or commendation herein, let him command me on the contrary, if he thinks me blame worthy, let him blame
me therefore, I just stop here for a while. This Brahmin is a great official of Magda and he addresses
our Buddha as Master Gautama because a Gautama is the surname of our Buddha and sometimes our
Buddha is a dress as Samana Gautama. Samana is a reclusive renunciation. So these four qualities which
the Brahmin sees is all marks of a great man, great man and the great wisdom. The first one he
is learning, presumably he is learning in the Brahmin scriptures and will need knowledge etc.
The second one he understands, whatever he hears he understands. The third thing he has a
very good memory. The fourth one in the business of a householder he is killed in the business
of a householder and these things he thinks would make a great man. Then the Buddha said,
well Brahmin, and neither command you nor blame you herein. I myself proclaim a man possessed
of four qualities to be one of great wisdom, to be a great man. What are the four? Herein Brahmin,
we have a man given up to the welfare of many four, to the happiness of many four. By him
are many four established in the Aryan knowledge to wit, in what is of a lovely nature, in
one is of a profitable nature. Then the second one, to whatsoever train of thought he wishes to
apply himself, to that train of thought he applies himself, to whatever train of thought he desires
not to apply himself, to that train of thought he applies not himself. Whatever intention he wishes
to intend he does so or not if he so wishes does he is master of the mind in the ways of thought.
And the third one also he is one who attains that will without difficulty and without trouble.
The four genres which belong to the higher mind which even in this very life are blissful to
abide in. And the fourth also by destruction of the Asavas in this very life, thoroughly comprehending
it of himself. He realizes the liberation by mind, the liberation by wisdom and attaining it
a bite therein. No brahmin, neither command or blame you herein, but I myself proclaim a man
possessed of these four qualities to be one or great wisdom to be a great man. And these four
qualities that I put I mentioned, the first one is he is always looking after the welfare of many
fortmen to the many many people. He cares for the welfare of others. The second one is he is a master
of his mind to whatever train of thought he wishes to imply himself. He can do so. Whatever train
of thought he does not wish to apply himself. Also he can do so. And then the third one he attains
the four genres, the fourth one he has destroyed the Asavas. I like to mention again the first one
is this person he is always concerned with the welfare of others. But then in the sense that he
establishes people in the Aryan knowledge. That means he teaches the Aryan dharma to others.
This is what is meant by helping others. Now in the Buddha's teaching, the Buddha's
said, of all the gifts that we can make to others, the highest gift is the gift of the dharma,
the teaching of the truth. That is real compassion. Two only people like doing charity
and doing good deeds are becoming a vegetarian and all that now. That is good and it is good.
But then all these actions and the highest is the gift of knowledge. Because he we think about it carefully.
Suppose somebody is in difficulty and comes to us needs help. For example the person is hungry.
So if you are concerned with that person's welfare, you will give him food to eat and you
feed him. But after a few hours he is hungry again. Then you have to feed him again.
So tomorrow he is hungry again. So are you going to feed him the whole life? If you can do it, it is
very good. But then it will be more skillful of you. If you can teach him, for example, the trade
has given a work. So if he teaches, if he is taught now, a work where he can do to earn a living,
then you have no need to look after him day after day, then he will be able to take care of himself
and succeed in the world. But then that is just this lifetime he can earn a living. But then the next
lifetime he comes, he might suffer. Because he doesn't know how to walk the good path.
The path that we cultivate a lot of blessings to help us. And then because he has still continues
to be selfish, etc. Next life and he comes back, he will suffer again. But not be able to get food
again. But if we teach him the Dharma and then we teach him, make him understand that all these
coming obstructions that he is experiencing now is due to karma, past, deeds, for example, he has
been in selfish in this life or in the past life. And then because of that, not many people want to
help him. And then if he understands that he has to cultivate blessings so that people
can like him, to speak, kind words, his bitter, harsh words, etc. And then slowly, and he is also
got to understand that karma is not only past life, karma, but this life karma is got to exert
himself to look after himself in this life also. So he not only gets a job to
succeed in the world, we also cultivate some of the good qualities in life, which will help him
not only this life, even the next life. So we teach him this kind of Dharma, then we are benefiting
him not only this life, we are also benefiting him in the next life or the next few lives to come.
That's why the gift of Dharma is the highest gift. And because when we teach Dharma to people,
we are teaching them to lead a skillful life instead of an unskillful life.
The kusala and akusala, this word kusala is can be translated as skillful or wholesome,
wholesome life as skillful life. Because in life, we find suffering. There are two types of suffering.
There's a type of suffering which nobody can avoid. For example,
getting old, growing old, becoming sick and dying. Nobody can run away from that. All beings
would experience that. But there are certain kinds of suffering which are unnecessary, which we
kind of avoid. For example, if a person drinks and drives, you drink and drive again to accident,
there was not necessary. It could have been avoided. So in the same way, there are also other
things that we can cultivate. Now to avoid suffering, for example, if you speak skillful words,
to people instead of harsh words, then we can avoid unnecessary suffering in one way.
Another one is we know how to keep our precepts. That one, if you know how to listen to the Dharma,
if you know how to do charity, etc. So these are all the teachings that the Dharma teaches us
to lead a very skillful life so that we go to life with the minimum suffering. And not only that,
even the minimum suffering, eventually we should make it dry out by getting out of the
round of people. Then the second quality is that person is a master of his mind. He is so,
his mind is so well developed that he does not control by the mind. He controls the mind. A lot of
people, the melody. And then after many years of meditation, they think they are very skillful.
They think they have attained great stages. But then you can see their likes and dislikes. If they
have very strong likes and dislikes, that means they have not developed mind. If we have a
developed mind, we would not be controlled by our likes and dislikes. And then the third one is the
Janas, the condition for wisdom is the concentration. And the fourth one is destruction of the
Asavas, a technique at a hand-boot. Then the Brahmin said, it is wonderful master Gautama.
It is marvelous master Gautama. How well this has been said by the worthy Gautama? I myself do
hold the worthy Gautama to be possessed of these same qualities. Indeed, the worthy Gautama is given
up to the welfare of many folk, to the happiness of many folk. By him, I am many folk established
in the Aryan knowledge. To it, in what is of a lovely nature, in what is of a profitable nature.
Indeed, the worthy Gautama. To whatever, three in of thought he wishes to apply himself. To that
train of thought, he applies himself. Surely the worthy Gautama is master of the mind in the
ways of thought. Surely the worthy Gautama is one who attains that will the four Janas. Surely the
worthy Gautama by destruction of the Asavas, realizes the liberation by mind, the liberation by wisdom
and attaining it about staring. And the Buddha said, indeed Brahmin, your words come close and
challenge me to a statement. Nevertheless, I will satisfy you by replying. I am indeed given up
to the welfare of many folk, to the happiness of many folk. By me, I am many folk established in the
Aryan knowledge. To it, in what is of a lovely nature, in what is of a profitable nature. To
whatsoever train of thought, I desire to apply myself to that, I apply myself on not as I please.
Whatever intention I wish to intend, I do so on not as I please. Indeed, Brahmin, I master
I am master of the mind in the ways of thought. Indeed, I am one who attains that will
before Janas. Indeed, Brahmin by the destruction of the Asavas, I realize the liberation by mind
and liberation by wisdom and attaining it about there. And that's the end of the Suttaa here.
So, this is another case where somebody is challenging the Buddha. And he acknowledges that he has
attained all these vocalities.
